planting.note: Plant fresh nuts in fall at 1-2 inches depth in well-drained soil; hickory nuts need natural cold stratification over winter to germinate in spring. Black hickory develops a long taproot quickly, so transplant only small seedlings before the taproot becomes difficult to move.

directions: Plant in well-drained to dry, sandy, rocky, or red clay soils in full sun; black hickory is the most drought-tolerant hickory native to Texas and the Coastal Plain, naturally inhabiting dry sandy uplands, rocky ridges, and sandstone hills where other hickories fail. The nuts are small and have thick shells relative to their edible kernel, but are consumed by squirrels, deer, wild turkeys, and other wildlife that provide the ecological services of seed dispersal. The dark, rough, blocky bark becomes increasingly distinctive with age. Like all hickories, black hickory is very slow to establish (most growth goes underground in early years as the taproot develops) but eventually becomes a long-lived, tough, low-maintenance tree well-suited to the challenging dry sandy soils of east Texas and Oklahoma. Important wildlife food tree for dry upland woodland habitats.

planting.note: Plant fresh nuts in fall at 1-2 inches depth in moist, well-drained to wet soil; bitternut hickory naturally grows in moist bottomlands and slopes and is one of the fastest-growing and most adaptable hickories. Seeds require natural cold stratification over winter.

directions: Plant in moist to well-drained, fertile, loamy or clay soil in full sun; bitternut hickory is the most widely distributed hickory in eastern North America and one of the fastest growing, making it valuable as a canopy tree where time to a large shade tree is a priority. The nuts are notably bitter and unpalatable to humans (hence the common name), being too high in tannins for human consumption even after shelling — unlike the sweet-nutted shagbark and shellbark hickories. However, they are consumed by squirrels, deer, and wood ducks after additional caching and processing. The distinctive butter-yellow winter buds are an important winter identification trait. The golden-yellow fall color is attractive, and the tree provides superb canopy for native plant understories. One of the most moisture-tolerant hickories.

planting.note: Plant fresh nuts in fall at 2-3 inches depth in well-drained, alkaline to neutral soil; Texas black walnut grows in challenging, dry, rocky, alkaline limestone soils where eastern black walnut fails. Squirrels often cache and forget nuts, naturally regenerating the species.

directions: Plant in well-drained, rocky, chalky, or limestone-derived soil in full sun in central and south Texas; Texas black walnut is the small, drought-tolerant walnut native to the rocky limestone hills and creek drainages of central Texas, Oklahoma, and New Mexico. Unlike the towering eastern black walnut (J. nigra), this species stays at 20-30 feet with a spreading, multi-stemmed habit. The small nuts are round and dark-shelled with a proportionally small but edible, walnut-flavored kernel — historically gathered and cracked by Indigenous peoples and early settlers. The tree produces juglone in its roots and leaves like all walnuts, inhibiting the growth of some nearby plants, especially tomatoes, peppers, and certain other species. An excellent native tree for dry, rocky, alkaline soils of the Texas Hill Country and adjacent areas where eastern black walnut cannot thrive.

planting.note: Plant fresh nuts in fall at 2 inches depth in well-drained, acidic, sandy to loamy soil; Japanese chestnut naturally cold-stratifies over winter and germinates in spring. Plant two or more seedlings for cross-pollination and heavier nut production.

directions: Plant in well-drained, acidic, sandy to loamy, low-phosphorus soil in full sun; Japanese chestnut is critically important in chestnut breeding programs as a source of resistance to chestnut blight (Cryphonectria parasitica), the fungal disease that virtually eliminated the American chestnut from eastern North American forests in the early 20th century. While Japanese chestnut is not immune to the blight, it carries resistance genes that allow infected trees to survive and produce nuts despite infection — a trait intensively incorporated into breeding lines for blight-resistant American chestnut restoration. The nuts are large (often larger than American chestnut), sweet, and excellent roasted or boiled in Japanese cuisine. Japanese chestnut has been cultivated in Japan for over 2000 years and is the source of many cultivated varieties. Requires cross-pollination; plant two or more trees.

planting.note: Plant fresh nuts in fall at 2 inches depth in well-drained, acidic, rocky to loamy soil; Ozark chinkapin grows in dry, rocky, ridge-top conditions and requires good drainage. Plant two trees for cross-pollination.

directions: Plant in well-drained, acidic, rocky or loamy soil in full sun; Ozark chinkapin is a native chestnut relative endemic to the Ozark Highlands of Arkansas and Missouri, historically less severely impacted by chestnut blight than the eastern American chestnut and still producing viable nuts in remnant populations. The tree occupies a niche on dry, rocky, acidic Ozark ridges where it was a valuable food resource for both wildlife and people — the sweet, thin-shelled nuts were gathered and eaten raw, roasted, and dried for winter storage. Like other American Castanea species, it is threatened by chestnut blight, though some individual trees show variable resistance. Conservation organizations actively collect seeds from resistant individuals for propagation and restoration. The nuts are smaller than Japanese chestnut but excellent in flavor, with a rich, sweet taste.
